title: [lichen_l2.c:744] Logs sensitive data (EUI-64) after zeroing
description:
What: lichen_peer_remove() logs the peer's EUI-64 address after calling secure_zero() on it. The LOG_INF at line 744 reads eui64[6] and eui64[7], but secure_zero() was called at line 742.

Why it matters: Reading memory after secure_zero() produces undefined values (zeros). The log message will always show '..00:00' rather than the actual removed peer's address. This is a logic error, not a security issue.

Evidence:
```c
secure_zero(entry->pubkey, sizeof(entry->pubkey));
secure_zero(entry->eui64, sizeof(entry->eui64));  // zeroes eui64
entry->active = false;
LOG_INF("lichen_l2: peer removed ..%02x:%02x",
        eui64[6], eui64[7]);  // reads function param eui64, not entry->eui64
```

Wait, this actually reads the function parameter 'eui64' not 'entry->eui64'. Let me re-verify...
close_reason: False positive: The LOG_INF reads the function parameter 'eui64' (const pointer to caller's data), not 'entry->eui64' which was zeroed. The log is correct.
